chapters.title: Linear Functions
chapters.desc: Functions, slope, and linear modeling — plus the Excel moves (formulas, cell references, basic graphs) you'll lean on the rest of the term.
chapters.intro_html:
This week is about three ideas that show up everywhere once you notice them: functions, slope, and linear modeling. By Sunday you'll write functions, read slope from a table, and use a linear equation to predict numbers your gut already half-knows.

You'll also start using Excel. The Excel moves are small but they make every other week of this course possible. If Excel feels rusty, lean on the Scribe walkthroughs.
objectives: Explore the basics of functions and their uses.
Use spreadsheet applications: functions, cell references, and basic graphing.
Use rounding and estimation to solve application problems.
Apply deductive and inductive reasoning to solve problems.
Define the slope as a rate of change.
Use linear modeling to predict outcomes from data.

glossary.definition: The rule itself. We name it with a letter, usually f, but g and h show up too.
The value you feed in. Almost always called x.
What comes out. Often called y, or written as f(x).
Read out loud as "f of x." It means: "the output of the function f when the input is x." It does not mean f times x.
To compute a function's output for a specific input. "Evaluate f at x = 7" means: substitute 7 wherever you see x in the rule, then simplify. Same idea as plugging a value in.
All inputs that are allowed. (Working 0 hours makes sense. Working −5 hours doesn't.)
All possible outputs you can actually get out.
Top-down. Apply a general rule to a specific case. "All large drinks are $5.50. This is large. So it costs $5.50."
Bottom-up. Generalize a pattern from specific examples. "My large drink has been $5.50 every time. Probably it'll be $5.50 today."
A pattern-based guess from inductive reasoning. Not yet proven, but reasonable.
A single case that breaks a conjecture. One counterexample is enough, it only takes one black swan to disprove "all swans are white."
A starting statement assumed true. Deductive reasoning chains premises to a conclusion.
What you end up with. In deduction, it's certain. In induction, it's probable.

A digit's position. In 4,587: 4 is thousands, 5 is hundreds, 8 is tens, 7 is ones. To the right of the decimal point, the places keep going: in 7.4839, the 4 is tenths, 8 is hundredths, 3 is thousandths, 9 is ten-thousandths.

Replace a number with the nearest higher or lower "nice" value. Look at the digit just to the right of your target place, 5 or more, round up; less than 5, round down. The rule is the same whether you're rounding 4,587 to the nearest hundred or 7.4839 to the nearest hundredth.
An approximate answer. Faster than the exact calculation, and good enough most of the time.
Numbers chosen because they're easy to combine in your head. 27 + 38 becomes 30 + 40 = 70.
The rough size of a number. Is the answer in the tens? Hundreds? Thousands? This is what your gut catches when something feels "way off."
A fast estimate you do after a calculation to confirm the answer isn't absurd. If your calculator says $4,892 for groceries, your sanity check should scream.
A number that describes how steep a line is and which direction it tilts. The letter m is the standard symbol, there's no deep reason for it, it's just tradition.
The vertical change between two points. Up is positive, down is negative.
The horizontal change between two points. Right is positive, left is negative.
The plain-English version of slope. "60 mph," "$15 per hour," "3 inches per year", all rates of change.
A Greek letter meaning "change in." So Δy / Δx reads as "change in y over change in x", slope, in two characters.
A point on a graph, written (x, y). Slope needs two of them, that's why the formula has subscripts 1 and 2.
An equation that describes how one quantity changes at a constant rate as another quantity changes. Always takes the form y = mx + b.
The format y = mx + b. It's called this because m is the slope and b is the y-intercept, both ingredients are right there in the equation, no rearranging needed.
Where the line crosses the y-axis, the value of y when x = 0. In word problems, it's the starting amount, base fee, or fixed cost.
The input. The thing you control or that "drives" the change. Goes on the x-axis.
The output. The thing that depends on the input. Goes on the y-axis.
Using your model to predict beyond your data. Powerful, but also where models go wrong if the underlying pattern changes.
A single box in the grid. Can hold text, a number, or a formula.
The address of a cell, like B2. Column letter first, row number second.
Anything that starts with =. Excel calculates the result. Examples: =A1+5, =B2*3, =A2*0.10+25.

A built-in shortcut, like =SUM(A1:A10), =AVERAGE(B2:B20), =SLOPE(y, x), or =INTERCEPT(y, x). Same framing as the math f(x) from Lesson 1 (give it inputs, get one output), but more specific: each Excel function is a named tool with a fixed behavior. Use them when you'd rather not write the math by hand.
Click a cell with a formula, grab the small square at its corner, drag it down. Excel copies the formula to every cell you drag over. adjusting cell references automatically.

A best-fit line drawn through scattered data points on a chart. Right-click a data series → Add Trendline → choose Linear. Check Display Equation on chart to see y = mx + b printed alongside it. The fastest way to read slope and intercept off real data.
A graph generated from your cells. Select the data, click Insert Chart, choose Scatter or Line. Excel draws it for you.

chapters.summary_html:
Key formulas

The moves to memorize. Each one shows up multiple times across the topic.

Function notation
f(x) = the rule applied to input x
To evaluate f(2), substitute 2 wherever you see x in the rule. f(x) = 3x + 5 means f(2) = 3(2) + 5 = 11. Same input, same output, every time — that's the definition of a function.
Slope (rate of change)
m = y2 − y1x2 − x1  =  riserun
Pick any two points on the line. Slope is "how much y changes for every one-unit change in x." Positive m = rising left-to-right; negative m = falling.
Slope-intercept form
y = mx + b
m is the slope (multiplier of x). b is the y-intercept (where the line crosses the y-axis). Plug both in and you've described a line.
Three forms of a linear equation
Slope-intercept:  y = mx + b
Point-slope:  y − y1 = m(x − x1)
Standard:  Ax + By = C
Slope-intercept is the easiest to graph. Point-slope is best when you have a slope and one point. Standard is what ALEKS sometimes asks you to convert TO or FROM.
Rounding
digit is 5 or more → round up
digit is 4 or less → round down
Find the rounding place first, then look ONE digit to its right. The digit to the right decides; the rest of the trailing digits don't matter.
Ratio
"A to B" → AB  then reduce by GCF
Order matters. "Green to red" puts green on top, not red. After writing the ratio, reduce by dividing top and bottom by their greatest common factor (6/9 → 2/3).

Common mistakes

The traps that cost real points. Memorize these as much as the formulas.

  1. Calling a non-function a function. If one input maps to two different outputs (like {(1, 2), (1, 5)}), it's NOT a function. Same x = same y, every time.
  2. Looking at the wrong digit when rounding. "Round to the tenth" means look at the hundredths digit (the one after tenths). Identify the rounding place first, then look one step right.
  3. Slope sign mistakes. A line that falls left-to-right has NEGATIVE slope. If your computed slope is positive but the line clearly falls, you swapped (y2−y1) order.
  4. Rise/run reversed. Rise is the vertical change (Δy). Run is the horizontal change (Δx). If your slope is "1/4" but should be "4," check which one you put on top.
  5. Confusing slope and y-intercept. In y = 2x + 5, slope is 2 and y-intercept is 5. The slope is the multiplier of x; the intercept is the constant added on.
  6. Plotting slope wrong from the intercept. Start at the y-intercept, then go UP THE RISE and OVER THE RUN to find a second point. Negative slopes go DOWN and over.
  7. Ratio in the wrong order. "Green to red" is green/red, not red/green. Read the prompt twice before writing.
  8. Forgetting to reduce a fraction. 6/9 is correct as a raw ratio but if the prompt says "lowest terms," divide top and bottom by their GCF: 2/3.
  9. Function evaluation parentheses. f(2x) is NOT 2·f(x). Substitute carefully — wherever you see x in the rule, replace it with the entire input.
  10. Excel: forgot the = sign. Every formula in Excel starts with =. A2+B2 is just text; =A2+B2 is the calculation.

Quick reference

The visual + Excel patterns worth keeping at hand.

Special slopes
m = 0  →  horizontal line (no rise)
m = undefined  →  vertical line (no run, division by zero)
m > 0  →  line rises left-to-right
m < 0  →  line falls left-to-right
Reading slope from a graph or table
From a graph: pick two clear lattice points. Count rise (vertical) and run (horizontal). Slope = rise/run, with sign matching the direction.
From a table: for any two rows, slope = (Δy)/(Δx). If x-values are equally spaced, slope = the constant difference in y divided by that x-step.
Function vocabulary
Function — the rule, named with a letter (f, g, h)
Input — the value you feed in (typically x)
Output — what comes out (typically y or f(x))
Evaluate — plug a specific value in for x
Domain — the set of valid inputs
Range — the set of resulting outputs
Excel basics
=A2+B2  sum two cells
=SUM(A2:A10)  sum a range
=AVERAGE(B2:B10)  average a range
=SLOPE(y_range, x_range)  best-fit slope
=INTERCEPT(y_range, x_range)  best-fit y-intercept
$A$1  absolute reference (won't shift on copy)
Two ways to reason
Deductive — apply a known rule to get a guaranteed answer (the slope formula, plugging into a function)
Inductive — spot a pattern from examples and predict (figuring out a function rule from a table of values)

sections.title: What is a function?
sections.objective: Explore the basics of functions and their uses.
sections.deck: A function is one of the simplest, most useful ideas in math, and you've been using them your whole life without knowing the name.

sections.title: Two ways to reason
sections.objective: Apply deductive and inductive reasoning to solve problems.
sections.deck: Every problem you solve uses one of two reasoning moves. Knowing which is which makes you a sharper thinker, in math, and in everything else.

sections.title: Close enough
sections.objective: Use rounding and estimation to solve application problems.
sections.deck: Rounding makes hard numbers easy. Estimation strings them together to predict, and protect you from, every total you'll ever calculate.

sections.title: Slope as a rate of change
sections.objective: Define the slope as a rate of change.
sections.deck: Every "per" you've ever said (dollars per hour, miles per gallon, feet per second) is a slope. It's how mathematicians measure change.

sections.title: Linear modeling and prediction
sections.objective: Use linear modeling to predict outcomes from data.
sections.deck: A linear model is just slope plus a starting point. It's everything you've learned in this topic, packed into a single line of math, and it's how a number becomes a forecast.

sections.title: Spreadsheets, the finale
sections.objective: Use spreadsheet applications: functions, cell references, and basic graphing.
sections.deck: You learned the math. Now learn to automate it. A spreadsheet is just a giant grid of functions, and you already know how to write functions.

chapters.title: Conversions & Budgeting
chapters.desc: Percent, unit conversions, and building a budget you could actually use — anchored by the first Major Assignment.
chapters.intro_html:
This week is about three moves that show up in adult life everywhere: percent, conversions, and budgeting. By Sunday you'll compute tip and tax in your head, convert any unit to any other unit, and have a real budget you could plug your own numbers into.

The math is mostly arithmetic. What you're really learning is how to set things up — in your head, on paper, and in Excel — so the math works for you.
objectives: Solve application problems involving percent.
Convert units using dimensional analysis.
Convert between fractions, decimals, and percentages.
Use budgeting techniques to help develop financial literacy skills.

glossary.definition: A fraction whose denominator is 100. The % symbol means "divide by 100."
The whole, the 100% reference. In "$60, 25% off," the base is $60.
The piece you're computing or comparing to the base. In the same example, the part (the discount amount) is $15.
A percent rewritten without the % sign. 25% = 0.25. Always shift the decimal two places to the left.
A ratio: top over bottom (numerator over denominator). 1/4 means 1 part out of 4 equal parts.
Place value to the right of the decimal point: tenths, hundredths, thousandths. 0.25 means two tenths plus five hundredths.
The move you make to rewrite a number from one form to another. The recipes: fraction to decimal = divide top by bottom; decimal to percent = multiply by 100; percent to decimal = divide by 100.
An increase added to a base price. A store buys an item for $20, marks it up 50%, and sells it for $30.
A decrease taken off a base price. A $60 sweater on sale at 30% off costs $42.
A percent added to a restaurant bill, usually 15%-20% in the U.S. Math is the same as sales tax.
A percent added to a purchase price by the state or city. Rates vary, often between 5% and 9%.
The catch-all term for any move that increases or decreases a base by a percent. ALEKS uses this language often.
A fraction whose numerator and denominator are equivalent quantities in different units, e.g. 5280 ft / 1 mi or 2.54 cm / 1 in. Always equals 1.
The formal name for this technique. Set up a chain of conversion factors so the original unit cancels and the target unit survives.
Top of the fraction / bottom of the fraction. Which slot a unit lives in determines whether it cancels or survives.
Two measurements that name the same physical amount: 12 inches and 1 foot, 1000 grams and 1 kilogram, 4 quarts and 1 gallon.
Two or more conversion factors multiplied in a row. Cancellation propagates link by link until the surviving unit matches the target.
A unit that's a ratio of two units: mph (miles/hour), $/gal (dollars/gallon), kg/L (kilograms/liter). Convert the top and the bottom separately.
Just unit conversion with money. The exchange rate IS the conversion factor. 1 USD = 0.92 EUR means the factor is 0.92 EUR / 1 USD.
Money coming in: paycheck, side hustle, gifts. Use take-home pay (after tax) for budgeting, not gross.
Money going out, broken into categories. Rent, food, transport, subscriptions, discretionary spending. Each category is a line item.
Fixed expenses are the same every month (rent, phone bill, insurance). Variable expenses change (groceries, gas, eating out).
Savings as a percent of income. savings / income × 100%. Higher is better. 10-20% is solid for an early-career budget.

chapters.summary_html:
Key formulas

The moves to memorize. Each one shows up multiple times across the topic.

Percent ↔ decimal
shift the decimal two places: 25% ↔ 0.25
Decimal → percent: shift right (number gets bigger). Percent → decimal: shift left (number gets smaller). Always two places, never one.
Three percent shapes
Find the part: part = rate × base  (20% of 50 = 10)
Find the percent: rate = part ÷ base × 100  (12 is 24% of 50)
Find the whole: base = part ÷ rate  (8 is 25% of 32)
Always convert the percent to a decimal first. Always identify which piece is missing before you decide multiply vs. divide.
Multiplier shortcut
Percent ON: × (1 + rate)  |  Percent OFF: × (1 − rate)
Tip 18% → ×1.18. Sale 25% off → ×0.75. Stack moves on a single price: $120 × 0.75 × 1.07 = $96.30.
Unit conversion (multiply by 1)
A conversion factor (e.g. 5280 ft / 1 mi) equals 1.
Set it up so the unwanted unit cancels on the bottom.
Chain factors when one isn't enough: 5 mi × (5280 ft/mi) × (12 in/ft) = 316,800 in.
Temperature (the exception)
F = (9/5) × C + 32  |  C = (5/9) × (F − 32)
Different zero points means dimensional analysis breaks. Use the formula. Multiply BEFORE you add or subtract.
Budget
income = expenses + savings
savings rate = savings ÷ income × 100
10–20% savings rate is solid for an early-career budget.

Common mistakes

The traps that cost real points. Memorize these as much as the formulas.

  1. Forgetting to convert percent to decimal. 7% of 80 is 0.07 × 80 = 5.6, not 7 × 80 = 560. Always strip the % sign first.
  2. Shifting the decimal one place instead of two. 0.4 = 40%, not 4%. 28.5% = 0.285, not 2.85.
  3. Wrong direction on a discount. "30% off" means you PAY 70%, not 30%. The discount AMOUNT is 30% of price; the FINAL price is what you pay.
  4. Tax on the wrong price. When stacking discount + tax, tax applies to the SALE price, not the original. Discount first, then tax.
  5. Conversion factor upside down. If you got an answer in weird units (ft²/in or kg²/lb), the fraction was backwards. The unit you want to cancel goes on the BOTTOM.
  6. Compound units only converted on top. Converting mph to ft/sec needs BOTH conversions: mi → ft on top, hr → sec on the bottom. Don't forget the time piece.
  7. Squared units only converted once. in² to cm² needs the in/cm factor TWICE. The hint to look for: "one factor is applied twice."
  8. F↔C order of operations. Multiply by 9/5 (or 5/9) BEFORE adding or subtracting 32. The parentheses in the formula are not optional.
  9. Rounding too early. When ALEKS says "do not round," carry full precision through every step. Rounding 1.6 to 2 in a speed conversion will throw the answer off by 25%.
  10. Hand-typing numbers in Excel. Always reference cells (=A2*0.07 or =$B$1*A2) so the formula updates if any input changes. The grader and your future self will thank you.

Quick reference

Conversion factors worth memorizing. ALEKS gives you these in the prompt, but knowing them speeds you up.

Distance
1 mi = 5,280 ft  ·  1 ft = 12 in  ·  1 yd = 3 ft
1 in = 2.54 cm  ·  1 m = 3.28 ft  ·  1 km = 0.621 mi
Weight
1 lb = 16 oz  ·  1 T = 2,000 lb  ·  1 kg = 2.2 lb
1 oz ≈ 28.35 g  ·  1 g = 1,000 mg
Volume
1 gal = 4 qt  ·  1 qt = 2 pt  ·  1 cup = 8 fl oz
1 L ≈ 33.81 fl oz  ·  1 L ≈ 0.264 gal  ·  1 tsp ≈ 4.93 mL
Time
1 d = 24 h  ·  1 h = 60 min  ·  1 min = 60 sec
1 h = 3,600 sec  ·  1 yr = 365 d
Common percents
50% = 0.5 = 1/2  ·  25% = 0.25 = 1/4  ·  75% = 0.75 = 3/4
20% = 0.2 = 1/5  ·  33.3% ≈ 0.333 = 1/3  ·  10% = 0.1 = 1/10

sections.title: What is a percent, really?
sections.objective: Solve application problems involving percent.
sections.deck: Percent is just a fancy way of saying "out of 100." Once that clicks, every percent problem becomes the same simple recipe.

sections.title: Three masks, one number.
sections.objective: Convert between fractions, decimals, and percentages.
sections.deck: Fractions, decimals, and percents are three ways to write the same number. Once you can switch between them on demand, you can pick the form that makes the math easiest.

sections.title: Percent in the wild.
sections.objective: Apply percent to real-world increase, decrease, tip, and tax.
sections.deck: Tip, sales tax, raises, discounts, markup. Different stories, same recipe. This lesson teaches you the move and the one-step shortcut that pays off all term.

sections.title: Multiply by 1, in disguise.
sections.objective: Convert units using dimensional analysis.
sections.deck: Every unit conversion uses the same elegant move: multiply by a fraction equal to 1, written so the unwanted unit cancels. Once you see it, you can convert anything.

sections.title: Chain the move.
sections.objective: Chain conversions and convert compound units.
sections.deck: When one factor isn't enough, stack several. Each one cancels a unit until what's left matches your target. Same recipe as Lesson 4, scaled up.

sections.title: Tell your money what to do.
sections.objective: Use budgeting techniques to develop financial literacy skills.
sections.deck: A budget is a math instrument. It tells your dollars where to go before they vanish. Once you set it up in a spreadsheet, the percent and conversion tricks from this whole topic do the heavy lifting.

chapters.title: Savings
chapters.desc: Simple vs. compound interest, the CPI and inflation, and annuity formulas for planning how money grows over time.
chapters.intro_html:
This week is about how money grows over time. By Sunday you'll know the difference between simple and compound interest, how an annuity adds up over years of monthly deposits, and why starting earlier matters more than saving more.

You'll meet three formulas you'll use the rest of the course: simple interest (FV = P(1+rt)), compound interest (FV = P(1+r/n)nt), and annuity future value. The shapes look heavy on the page; the moves are small.
objectives: Use simple and compound interest formulas to analyze financial issues.
Explore the relationship between the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and inflation.
Use the lump sum and annuity formulas to explore methods of financial savings.
Analyze systems of investments to help develop financial literacy skills.

glossary.definition: The starting amount: what you borrowed or deposited. Every formula starts here.
The annual interest rate, written as a decimal. 5% becomes 0.05 before it goes into a formula.
The term of the loan or investment, in years. Six months is 0.5; a decade is 10.
The dollar amount of interest itself — the growth in the pile, separate from the principal.
The whole pile after interest: principal plus interest. Most ALEKS questions ask for A, not I.
Interest computed only on the original principal. Doesn't compound.
Equal additions in equal time periods. The graph is a straight line.
Short-term U.S. government debt — typically 4, 13, 26, or 52 weeks. Standard real-world use of simple interest.
How long the principal is lent or invested, in years. Convert months and days to years before plugging into the formula.
Principal plus interest. A = P + I, which factors to P(1 + rt).
Interest paid on principal plus previously earned interest. The pile grows on itself.
The interval at which interest is added to the principal. Annual, monthly, daily, or continuous.
Number of compounding periods per year. Annual = 1, semiannual = 2, quarterly = 4, monthly = 12, daily = 365.
The limit case where interest is added instantaneously and continuously. Uses A = Pert.
An irrational constant, approximately 2.71828. Appears whenever something grows or decays continuously.
What an account actually pays in a year, with compounding factored in. Always ≥ APR for any n > 1.
The advertised annual rate, before compounding effect. Also called the stated or nominal rate.
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics index tracking the cost of a basket of household goods. The data source for inflation.
The annual percent increase in prices. Computed from CPI: (CPInew / CPIold) − 1.
Nominal is the headline rate. Real is what's left after subtracting inflation. Real is what your purchasing power actually did.
One deposit, left to grow. Future value comes from the compound interest formula.
A series of equal periodic payments — weekly, monthly, or annually — into a savings or investment account.
The amount of each regular deposit. The ALEKS dictionary uses M; some textbooks use PMT.
How long the money has to grow. Lengthening t beats raising r for long-term saving.
3 to 6 months of expenses, kept liquid. The first savings goal — before any longer-term investing makes sense.
An online savings account paying APYs near the federal funds rate. Where short-term savings live.
A time deposit with a fixed term and rate. Can't withdraw early without penalty. Used for mid-term goals.
Retirement accounts with tax advantages. Where long-term savings compound for decades. Topic 7 covers what to put inside them.

chapters.summary_html:
Compute, don't memorize

Plug in your numbers and get the answer in either direction. The web tool solves for any variable; the Excel workbook is yours to keep.

▸ Open the Financial Formulas Calculator ▼ Download the Excel workbook

Key formulas (ALEKS dictionary)

The exact formulas the ALEKS test expects. Variable names match the on-screen reference so you can jump from this page to the dictionary without re-mapping anything.

Simple interest
I = Prt  ·  A = P(1 + rt)
No compounding. Use for short-term loans, T-bills, and most ALEKS warmup problems.
Compound interest
A = P(1 + r/n)nt
Discrete compounding. n = periods per year (annual = 1, monthly = 12, daily = 365).
Continuous compounding
A = Pert
The limit of compound interest as n → ∞. e ≈ 2.71828 is Euler's number.
Future value of an annuity
A = M[(1 + r/n)nt − 1] / (r/n)
A series of equal payments M compounded over nt periods. Use for monthly contributions to a retirement account.
Effective annual yield (APY)
Y = (1 + r/n)n − 1
What a savings account actually pays in a year, with compounding factored in. Use APY (not APR) to compare two accounts.
Loan amortization → Topic 4
M = P(r/12) / (1 − (1 + r/12)−12t)
In the dictionary but lives in Topic 4 (Loans) — see that topic's cheat sheet for the worked version.

Variable legend

Same names as the ALEKS dictionary so the formulas above read identically on the test.

P = principal  ·  I = simple interest  ·  r = annual interest rate (decimal)
t = time, in years  ·  A = future value or amount accumulated
n = compounding periods per year  ·  e = Euler's number (≈ 2.71828)
M = installment payment or periodic deposit  ·  Y = effective annual yield (APY)

Common compounding values of n

annually = 1  ·  semiannually = 2  ·  quarterly = 4
monthly = 12  ·  weekly = 52  ·  daily = 365
continuously → use A = Pert instead

Common slips

Forgot to convert the rate
5% goes into the formula as 0.05, not 5. Always.
Mixed up A and I
I is just the interest, A is principal + interest. ALEKS questions usually ask for A.
Months instead of years
t is in years. 18 months is 1.5, not 18. Convert before plugging in.
Compared APRs instead of APYs
Two accounts with different compounding schedules need APY for a fair compare. APR alone hides the compounding difference.

sections.title: What interest actually is.
sections.objective: Set up interest problems by identifying principal, rate, and time, and distinguish simple interest from the total accumulated amount.
sections.deck: Before any formula, four words: principal, rate, time, interest. Once you name them, every calculation in this topic becomes a recipe.

sections.title: Simple interest, the warmup formula.
sections.objective: Apply the simple interest formula I = Prt and the accumulated-amount formula A = P(1 + rt) to short-term lending and saving problems.
sections.deck: Multiply principal by rate by time. That's it. Simple interest grows in a straight line — no compounding, no surprises.

sections.title: Compound interest, and where it leads.
sections.objective: Apply the compound interest formula at any compounding frequency, and recognize continuous compounding as its limit.
sections.deck: Interest earning interest. Run the same formula at four different compounding frequencies and watch the values converge — toward continuous compounding, the limit case.

sections.title: The rate you really earn.
sections.objective: Compute the effective annual yield (APY) and use CPI data to find the real (inflation-adjusted) return on a savings account.
sections.deck: The advertised rate isn't what you get. APY adjusts for compounding frequency. Real return adjusts for inflation. Two flavors of the same insight.

sections.title: Lump sum vs. annuity.
sections.objective: Use the lump-sum and annuity future-value formulas to project savings forward across long time horizons.
sections.deck: One deposit or many. Both grow with compound interest, but the formulas look different because of how the deposits stack up over time.

sections.title: Build a savings plan.
sections.objective: Plan for short-, medium-, and long-term financial goals using lump-sum and annuity formulas inside savings vehicles.
sections.deck: Three goals, three formulas, three time horizons. Emergency fund, mid-term goals, retirement — each one a savings problem with the math you've already learned.

chapters.title: Loans
chapters.desc: Short- and long-term loans — auto loans, credit cards, student loans, and mortgages — plus an APA-style written analysis. The heaviest week of the course.
chapters.intro_html:
This week is loans. The flip side of savings: instead of money growing for you, money is growing against you on a balance you owe. By Sunday you'll be able to read a monthly payment, total cost, and total interest for any fixed-rate loan — auto, credit card, student, or mortgage.

The central formula is the amortization formula: M = P(r/12) / (1 − (1+r/12)−12t). Once you can read it, every loan in your life becomes a known quantity. Topic 3 was the math of growth; Topic 4 is the math of paying it back.
objectives: Compute monthly payments and interest costs of short-term loans such as auto loans or credit cards.
Find the interest, the balance due, and the minimum monthly payment for loans.
Analyze the various aspects of long-term loans such as student loans and home mortgages.

glossary.definition: The amount you borrowed — the loan amount. Sticker price minus any down payment.
The annual interest rate as a decimal. 6.5% becomes 0.065. The amortization formula uses r/12 internally to convert to a monthly periodic rate.
The length of the loan in years. Auto loans are usually 3-7; mortgages are 15 or 30; credit cards have no fixed term.
The fixed amount you pay each month. The amortization formula in Lesson 2 computes this from the other three pieces.
To pay off a loan in fixed payments over time. Each payment is interest first, principal second.
The expression above. The only formula in the ALEKS dictionary not introduced in T3.
The monthly interest rate. Annual rate divided by 12 because there are 12 monthly compounding periods per year.
The total number of monthly payments over the life of the loan. A 5-year auto loan has 60 payments; a 30-year mortgage has 360.
(1 + r/12)−12t = 1 / (1 + r/12)12t. Most calculators handle the minus sign correctly; in Excel the syntax is ^(-12*t).
Total cost of a loan = M × 12 × t (every payment summed). Total interest = total cost − principal. Both grow fast as t increases.
A fixed-term loan to buy a vehicle. Term typically 3-7 years; rate depends on credit score and current rates.
Money paid up front. Reduces the principal you finance and the monthly payment that results. Usually 10-20% of sticker price.
The full price of the vehicle. Equals down payment plus the loan amount.
The principal of the loan. Sticker price minus down payment. This is the P that goes into the amortization formula.
The loan duration in years. Longer term means lower monthly payment but more total interest paid.
A loan with no fixed term — you can borrow more, pay down, repeat. Credit cards and home-equity lines of credit are the common examples.
The annual rate the card charges on unpaid balances. Typical range: 18-29%. Divide by 12 for the monthly periodic rate.
The smallest payment the card issuer requires each month. Usually 1-3% of balance, plus the interest charged that month. Pays the loan off agonizingly slowly.
The part of your payment that covers the month's interest charge. Computed first; what's left goes to principal.
The part of your payment that actually reduces the balance. Equals payment − interest portion.
Federal loan where the government pays interest during in-school years and deferment. Only available to students with demonstrated financial need.
Federal loan where interest accrues from disbursement. Available regardless of need.

Accrued in-school interest gets added to the principal at the start of repayment, increasing the amount you'll pay back. ALEKS uses simple interest (I = Prt) for the in-school accrual; real federal loans accrue daily.
A pause in repayment (e.g. while in school). Subsidized loans don't accrue interest during deferment; unsubsidized loans do.

Standard: 10-year fixed term, computed by the L2 formula. IBR (income-driven): monthly payment is a percent of discretionary income, term up to 25 years, with possible forgiveness of remaining balance.
A long-term loan secured by real estate. Standard terms are 15 or 30 years; the home itself is collateral.
How each monthly payment breaks down. Front-loaded toward interest in early years, back-loaded toward principal in later years.
A row-by-row table of every payment, the interest portion, the principal portion, and the remaining balance. The full visual record of how the loan gets paid off.
An account the lender holds to pay your property taxes and homeowner's insurance. You pay 1/12 of the annual amount each month as part of your mortgage payment.
Principal + Interest + Taxes + Insurance — the four components of a typical mortgage payment. When people say "my mortgage" they usually mean PITI.
Replacing an existing mortgage with a new one, usually at a lower rate. Resets the amortization clock.

chapters.summary_html:
Compute, don't memorize

Plug in your numbers and get the answer in either direction. The web tool solves for any variable; the Excel workbook is yours to keep.

▸ Open the Financial Formulas Calculator ▼ Download the Excel workbook

Key formulas

The amortization formula is the only ALEKS dictionary formula we didn't already use in T3. Everything else is per-payment arithmetic built on top of it.

Loan amortization (the central T4 formula)
M = P(r/12) / (1 − (1 + r/12)−12t)
P = loan amount, r = annual rate (decimal), t = years. Output: fixed monthly payment M.
Total cost & total interest
total cost = M × 12 × t
total interest = total cost − P
A 30-year mortgage at 7% on $300K pays roughly $419K in interest alone — more than the loan itself.
Per-payment split (mortgage / amortization schedule)
interest portion = balance × (r/12)
principal portion = M − interest portion
new balance = balance − principal portion
Apply once for ALEKS Q5 (one mortgage payment). Apply 360 times to build a 30-year amortization schedule.
Credit card statement (one statement period)
interest = balance × (r/12)
new balance = balance + interest − payment
Same per-payment split as a mortgage, but applied to a revolving balance with no fixed term. Minimum payments are typically 1-3% of balance, often barely covering the interest charge.
Down payment & loan amount
loan amount = sticker price − down payment
loan amount also = down% × sticker (when given as %)
P in the amortization formula is the amount financed, not the sticker price. Auto loan: 10-20% down typical. Mortgage: 20% conventionally, less with PMI.
Carry-forward from Topic 3
A = P(1 + r/n)nt (compound interest)
Used in Lesson 5 for unsubsidized student loan interest capitalization. Run T3's compound formula across the in-school years to find the new P for the amortization formula. Full version on the Topic 3 cheat sheet.

Variable legend

P = principal (loan amount)  ·  r = annual rate (decimal)
t = term in years  ·  M = monthly payment
r/12 = monthly periodic rate  ·  12t = total monthly payments
balance = remaining principal at any point during the loan

Common slips

Forgot the negative exponent
The formula has (1 + r/12)−12t in the denominator. In Excel that's (1+r/12)^(-12*t). Drop the minus sign and the answer is wildly off.
Used the sticker price as P
P is the amount financed, not the sticker price. Subtract the down payment first.
Used annual rate where monthly was needed
In credit card and mortgage per-payment split: interest = balance × (r/12), not balance × r. The per-period rate is monthly.
Computed total cost as just M × t (missing × 12)
There are 12 payments per year. Total cost = M × 12 × t, not M × t.

sections.title: What a loan is, and what amortization means.
sections.objective: Identify the four components of a loan (principal, annual rate, term, monthly payment) and explain how amortization gradually pays down principal over time.
sections.deck: Before any formula: four pieces (P, r, t, M) and a concept (amortization). A loan is principal you pay back in fixed monthly chunks; each chunk is part interest, part principal, until the principal hits zero.

sections.title: The amortization formula, anatomized.
sections.objective: Apply the loan amortization formula to compute the monthly payment given principal, annual rate, and term, and recognize why the formula has a negative exponent in its denominator.
sections.deck: M = P(r/12) / (1 − (1+r/12)^(−12t)). Five pieces, one negative exponent, and the whole topic depends on it. Walk through what each part does.

sections.title: Auto loans: the standard amortizing loan.
sections.objective: Compute monthly payment, total cost, and total interest for an auto loan; analyze the impact of term length and down payment on the loan.
sections.deck: Fixed monthly payment, fixed term (typically 3-7 years). Two new pieces enter: down payment, and the difference between sticker price and amount financed.

sections.title: Credit cards: the minimum-payment trap.
sections.objective: Compute the unpaid balance after a credit card payment and identify why minimum payments dramatically extend the time and total cost of paying off a balance.
sections.deck: Revolving credit, no fixed term. The monthly math is deceptively simple — interest portion + principal portion — but minimum payments at 24% APR can trap a $5,000 balance for decades.

sections.title: Student loans: subsidized, unsubsidized, repayment plans.
sections.objective: Compare subsidized and unsubsidized federal student loans, analyze the effect of interest capitalization on the monthly payment, and identify the standard repayment options.
sections.deck: Federal student loans amortize the same way as auto loans, with two twists: interest can accrue during school (unsubsidized) or not (subsidized), and there are multiple repayment plans you can pick at payback.

sections.title: Mortgages: 30 years, dollar by dollar.
sections.objective: Analyze a mortgage payment as a split between interest and principal, explain how that split shifts over the life of the loan, and identify the major components of total homeownership cost beyond the loan itself.
sections.deck: The longest, biggest amortizing loan most people will ever sign for. The new math: the principal/interest split per payment, and what it means that early payments are mostly interest.

chapters.title: Probability
chapters.desc: Counting, permutations and combinations, theoretical and experimental probability, and expected value. The math of uncertainty — and the engine behind every insurance premium, lottery ticket, and game of chance.
chapters.intro_html:
This chapter is probability — the math of uncertainty. By the end you'll be fluent in the probability ratio (favorable / total), the fundamental counting principle that powers every "how many possible outcomes" question, permutations vs combinations (does order matter?), and the expected value machine that puts a fair price on any game of chance.

Chapter 6 is the second half of the data side of the course. Chapter 5 was descriptive — how to read a data set you already have. Chapter 6 is predictive — what to expect from a process whose outcome isn't decided yet. Together they give you the tools to read polls, studies, and games of chance with the same fluency a statistician has.
objectives: Compare, compute, and interpret theoretical and empirical probabilities.
Develop and use the fundamental counting principle.
Develop a procedure for finding expected value.
Compute and interpret expected values.

glossary.definition: Any process with an uncertain outcome — rolling a die, drawing a card, spinning a wheel, flipping a coin.
A single possible result of an experiment. A die roll has six outcomes; a card draw has 52.
The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment.
A subset of the sample space — the outcomes counted as "yes" for a given question.
For equally-likely outcomes, P(A) = n(A) / n(S), the count of favorable outcomes divided by the total. Always in [0, 1].
The event A<sup>c</sup> consisting of every outcome not in A. P(A<sup>c</sup>) = 1 − P(A).
For a multi-step process with independent steps, the total number of outcomes is the product of the number of choices at each step.
The event that A or B (or both) occurs. A ∪ B.
The event that A and B both occur. A ∩ B.
Two events that cannot both occur on the same trial. Their intersection is empty, so P(A ∩ B) = 0.
P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B). Subtract the overlap once to avoid double-counting.
Two events whose occurrence does not affect each other. For independent A and B, P(A ∩ B) = P(A) · P(B).
The product n · (n−1) · ... · 1. Counts the number of orderings of n distinct objects. 0! = 1 by convention.
Number of ordered selections of r items from n distinct items. P(n, r) = n! / (n − r)!.
Number of unordered selections of r items from n distinct items. C(n, r) = n! / [(n − r)! · r!].
A numerical quantity whose value depends on the outcome of a random experiment.
The probability-weighted average of a random variable: Σ x · P(x). The long-run average per trial.
A game with E(X) = 0; neither player nor opponent has a long-run advantage.
The proportion of trials, in a sequence of n repetitions of an experiment, on which the event occurred. Depends on the data, not the structure.
As the number of trials grows, the experimental probability of an event converges to the theoretical probability. Convergence, not equality.
Four suits (spades, clubs, hearts, diamonds), 13 ranks each. Hearts and diamonds are red; spades and clubs are black. Face cards are the J, Q, K (12 total).

sections.title: What probability is, and the favorable-over-total ratio
sections.objective: Compute the probability of a simple event as the ratio of favorable outcomes to total outcomes, and express the answer as a fraction in lowest terms, a decimal, or a percent.
sections.deck: Probability is a number between 0 and 1 that measures how likely an event is to occur. For simple events with equally-likely outcomes, the math reduces to one ratio: favorable outcomes over total outcomes.

sections.title: The fundamental counting principle
sections.objective: Apply the fundamental counting principle to determine the number of possible outcomes for a multi-step procedure by multiplying the number of choices at each step.
sections.deck: When a process has several independent steps, the total number of possible outcomes is the product of the number of choices at each step. The single most-used move in the topic.

sections.title: Sample spaces and the addition rule
sections.objective: Build a sample space for a single experiment and compute P(A or B) using the addition rule, including the correction for overlap when events are not mutually exclusive.
sections.deck: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B). Subtracting the overlap once prevents counting the shared outcomes twice. The standard 52-card deck is the canonical training ground.

sections.title: Permutations and combinations
sections.objective: Distinguish between permutations (order matters) and combinations (order does not), and apply the P(n, r) and C(n, r) formulas to standard selection problems.
sections.deck: When choices are made without replacement, the counting principle yields shrinking products. Permutations count ordered arrangements; combinations count unordered selections; the two differ by a factor of r!.

sections.title: Expected value
sections.objective: Compute the expected value of a discrete random variable as the sum of value × probability across all outcomes, and interpret the result as the long-run average per trial.
sections.deck: E(X) = Σ x · P(x). The single number that summarizes the long-run average payout of a random process. The math of fair games, insurance premiums, and casino edges.

sections.title: Theoretical and experimental probability
sections.objective: Compute both theoretical and experimental probability for compound independent events, compare the two, and describe how they converge as the number of trials increases (the Law of Large Numbers).
sections.deck: Theoretical probability comes from the structure of the experiment; experimental probability comes from the data of running it. The Law of Large Numbers says that as trials accumulate, the two converge.
